utterances: Up next, some lawmakers are pushing a bill that would require U.S.
intelligence agencies to take up a new task: analyzing how climate change could affect national security.
Think about it.
The proposed legislation comes on the heels of two recent reports that lay out this basic scenario.
You have rising temperatures worldwide that are projected to cause large-scale heat waves.
Seeing some of those heat waves already.
Droughts, we've got the droughts.
Australia has been in a drought for six years now.
You have rising sea levels, new disease outbreaks, which internal stress many poor and already vulnerable nations.

And that raises the risk of widespread civil and political conflict, which is certainly a matter of national security not only for those countries, but for countries like ours that deal with these issues.
So for the rest of the hour we're going to talk with the author of one of the new reports about the connection between climate change and national security.
If you'd like to give us a call, our number is 1-800-989-8255.
Peter Schwartz is the author of ""Inevitable Surprises: Thinking Ahead in a Time of Turbulence."" He's a co-founder and chairman of the Global Business Network in San Francisco.
Welcome back to the program, Peter.
Hi, Ira.
Glad to be back.
I don't know how much you - you have been listening to the program before?
Unfortunately, I couldn't listen, to be honest.
It wasn't broadcast here in Santa Fe.
That's right, you're in Santa Fe.
Well, let's talk about your report.
You know, you have been talking about this for a couple of years.
People are beginning to finally pay attention that climate change is - does have national security issues.
I think that's right.
I think the view of this has changed fundamentally.
Let me just say one thing, quickly, Ira, if I may - all your friends here at the Santa Fe Institute, all your fans say hi.
Okay, thank you.

Anyway, I think the climate for climate change has changed over the last few years in several very important respects that I think has changed the government's view and the public's view about the national security implications of this.
You know, I think, for example, Katrina was very significant in this regard.

Not that Katrina was caused by climate change, no one can say that, but the scale of the disaster, the consequences, the disruption to society, and for example, one of the most effective institutions that was involved in dealing with it was the U.S.
Coast Guard.
And so that became a kind of object lesson in some of the kind of consequences of climate change and the role of security agencies in dealing with it.
Well, let's talk about some other kinds of security concerns that a climate change would bring on.
Give us a little laundry list of the kinds of things that could happen.
Yes.
I was just grateful that it's not Shrimpche, right?

One of the things that's emerged in the last three days is that there really is a tangible feeling amongst the Sunni population of disenchantment about the failure so far to create a government which has, in the view of the Sunnis, adequate Sunni representation.
That feeling became very clear during Friday prayers in one of the largest Sunni mosques in Baghdad.

The imam there launched a tirade against the creation of this government, and the members of the congregation, for want of a better word, who were interviewed by a member of the NPR Iraqi staff afterwards, expressed deep bitterness about the failure so far to find adequate Sunni representation in the government.
That feeling came over again and again in interviews conducted there after Friday prayers.

So it is a real issue here and one that--it's clear that the prime minister, Ibrahim Al-Jafari, is going to have to tackle, and I'm sure most analysts would agree that he's going to have to tackle it quickly.
NPR's Philip Reeves in Baghdad, thank you.
You're welcome.
The Obama administration is also tackling the troubled economy.
Many economists say things could get worse before he ends his first term in office.
The latest unemployment numbers out today don't provide much comfort.
New claims for jobless benefits rose by 62,000 last week.
The current nationwide unemployment rate already stands at the highest level in 16 years.
Here to discuss what the Obama administration is up against in this economic climate is Simon Johnson.
He's a professor at MIT's Sloan School of Management.
And Professor Johnson, President Obama's team is talking about a big economic stimulus package to create jobs, so why are analysts predicting more job losses over the next four years?
Well, I think in part it sort of shows you how difficult the circumstances are, where we've got something akin to a catastrophe already under way in the economy.
And there's a limited amount that any government can do.
That's part of it.
In addition, we haven't seen the full Obama plan yet.
We know there's a large fiscal stimulus coming, and we're pretty sure there's going to be some help for housing, probably on a more modest scale.
But we don't know what they're going to do about the banks.
And until they resolve that uncertainty, it's hard to see the recovery really taking hold in any kind of sustained way that would be needed to bring down the unemployment rate on a big scale.
You've been writing about this.
And you've mentioned that even before we see any of the details of this plan, that there are certain constraints that the president is up against.
What are those?
Well, I think there are some political constraints and some economic constraints.
The main political constraint is, of course, he has to get a lot of buy-in on Capitol Hill.
Mr. Paulson made the mistake in September of trying to ram through Congress at very short notice what was regarded then as being a big program.
That created a lot of ill will and didn't help stabilize the financial situation.
On the economic side, there's a fair number of constraints.
I would say the key ones are that the banks need more capital, substantially more capital, and they probably also need a big cleanup of their balance sheets.
So if you do one without the other, you almost certainly don't get the full recovery that you're looking for.

Most people are trying to get a sense of just how bad the economy could get, and many are saying it won't be like the Great Depression, but the future may be a lot more like what happened in Japan in the late '80s and early 1990s.
Explain what happened then.

Well, in Japan they had something which they called the Golden Recession, which meant that output didn't go down much and unemployment actually, in the Japanese situation, didn't go up that much, but there was very little growth.
And every time it seemed like the economy was recovering, it just ran out of steam.
So they had 10 years or so - actually, it's probably more than that when you look back, like close to 15 - but it's called the lost decade.
And the heart of the problem was the Japanese consumers, and particularly Japanese firms, wanted to save more.
They got shocked and stretched by the bursting of a big real estate bubble.
And the conclusion they drew was they didn't want to borrow, they wanted to repair their balance sheets.
So, you had anemic demand, and there was very little the government could do about that.
The government tried.
The government did a lot of fiscal stimulus.
But even now, Japanese growth is lower than it should be.
So given the circumstances that we are facing here in the U.S.
now, what advice would you give the Obama administration?

Well, I think they need to take a little bit of time and put in place a very clear, strong, comprehensive plan which has a lot of the rules, if you like, laid out for what's going to happen in terms of banking system, restructuring, reorganization, recapitalization.
You need something big, decisive.
It needs to have a lot of political support.
So you really have to work Capitol Hill and build a lot of relationships.
And President Obama has to get out there, and he will get out there, and do a lot of explaining, both to politicians and to the public at large.
You've suggested that although it's necessary to create sustaining jobs, that the Obama administration shouldn't make job creation the priority when it comes to bank restructuring.
You said we need a complete change of management.
How do you suggest they go about doing that?
Well, I think that the government is going to have to get involved with ownership or quasi-ownership in these banks in return for the capital we've put in.
Look, the banks are basically out of capital.
They may be insolvent in some senses, the big banks.
And while depositors should be fully reassured that the FDIC stands behind their deposits - people are not going to lose money - the banks have clearly not been well run.
And in return for the government providing this capital, the government, I think, is going to get some form of control rights so they can sell to other people.
This so-called co-investment model in which you don't want the government to run the bank system, you want private sector to run it, but you want some different owners to come in.
And private equity is the most likely candidate.
Right.
Well, we wrestled over what great means.
Yes.
Private equity won't come in until they feel the banking situation and the overall macro economy have really turned the corner.
Private equity will come in, they'll clean up the banks, they'll fire a lot of managers, they will probably break them up.
I think that would be a good thing under the circumstances.

And we can move forward with a new, massively restructured banking system in which the taxpayer gets a fair amount of the upside, too, because we acquired common equity or warrants, options to buy common equity, that give you a good payoff when the assets and the banks recover.
Simon Johnson is a professor at MIT's Sloan School of Management.
Thanks.
Thank you.
By the end of this year, the federal government plans to removed gray wolves from the endangered species list in the northern Rocky Mountains.
That pleases many ranchers in Montana, Idaho, and Wyoming, who say the protected wolf population threatens their livelihood.
But as Shannon Mullen reports, the wolf comeback may be the least of ranchers' worries.
As the sun dips behind the Wind River Mountain Range in western Wyoming, long shadows fall across Stan Huvendick's(ph) 800-acre cattle ranch.
You see right out there, that of herd mulies there?
Last winter, there was 53 head of mules that wintered right there.
That's one of the reasons we ranch.
Looking our over his open range, Huvendick says the land hasn't changed much since pioneers followed the Oregon Trail through this area a century and a half ago.
But all around him in this valley, new houses are rising where cattle used to roam as more of his neighbors sell out of ranching.
And we've been offered real big money.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Like what?
Well, I mean, millions of millions of dollars.
But I don't want to sell.
What would I do?
Huvendick has been running cattle since 1965 and says it's never been profitable.
Now, he's one of a growing number of small-scale ranchers in the northern Rockies who claim they're barely staying in business.
Ask them why, the first thing they'll tell you is wolves.
With herds of fewer than 1,000 head, a loss of even one or two cattle can be financially crippling.
Grizzly bears and coyotes kill the most cattle, but more often, the wolf takes the blame.
He's a magnificent animal, and that's the way the good Lord made him.
If the wolf don't bother me, I don't bother him.
But if he's killing my cattle, I've got to do something.
We can't take the death loss that we've had, especially this year.
Since last winter, Huvendick says he's lost 16 cattle worth some $14,000.
But he admits he can't prove wolves kill them, and the number of confirmed livestock losses to wolves is small, 169 total this year out of more than six million cattle in the region.
To the individual rancher, it could be a big deal.
To a livestock industry, it's absolutely insignificant.
That's Ed Banks.
He's the regional wolf population manager for the U.S.
Fish and Wildlife Service.
He says the agency has used its authority to destroy 172 wolves this year in an aggressive response to livestock kills.
But he says that policy is not enough for ranchers.

The West is changing, and so the wolf becomes a symbol of the billionaires moving in to Jackson Hole and buying some big fancy ranch, so they can walk around and be a cowboy, and it has nothing to do with wolves.
Instead, it's about what kind of West this will become, whether tourist dollars and oil derricks would drive the economy instead of tractors, and working ranches will be endangered instead of wolves.
